ontology.foodex2_description: Saccharin and its salts (food additive / sweetener)

serving.natural_language_portion: A single saccharin tablet or drop weighs approximately 0.05 g and is used as a non-caloric sweetener substitute for one teaspoon of sugar.

nutrient_source.title: Saccharin — Singapore HPB Foodo Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for saccharin (food code F13020002). Values reflect the commercial saccharin powder/tablet product including bulking agents (dextrose/lactose), not pure saccharin compound. Per-100g figures are therefore high in sugar and energy due to bulking agents, not the saccharin molecule itself.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — dextrose/glucose-based bulking agent analogue
gi_source.source_match_notes: Pure saccharin has no glycemic index as it contains no digestible carbohydrate. The commercial saccharin product (HPB F13020002) contains dextrose and lactose as bulking agents contributing 89.11 g carbohydrate per 100 g. GI of 65 is estimated using dextrose-based bulking agent analogue from the University of Sydney GI Database. However, at a realistic serving size of 0.05 g per tablet, the glycemic load per serving is negligible at approximately 0.03 g, rendering the GI clinically irrelevant at typical use quantities. GL = (65 × 0.0446 g available carbohydrate per 0.05 g serving) / 100 ≈ 0.03.

hypertension.key_factor: Negligible sodium per serving despite high per-100g sodium from sodium saccharin salt form
hypertension.guidance: At a typical serving of 0.05 g per tablet, actual sodium intake is approximately 0.2 mg — clinically negligible — making saccharin tablets safe for individuals with hypertension when used as directed; however, patients should avoid high-dose or excessive use and monitor total dietary sodium from all sources.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Non-caloric sweetener with negligible glycemic load per serving; per-100g sugar figures reflect bulking agents, not saccharin itself
type2_diabetes.guidance: Saccharin is a non-caloric artificial sweetener approved by the American Diabetes Association for use in Type 2 Diabetes management; at typical tablet doses of 0.05 g, actual sugar and glycemic load delivered are negligible, though individuals should monitor gut microbiome response and prefer whole-food dietary strategies for long-term glucose control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Zero fat, zero saturated fat, zero cholesterol; negligible caloric contribution per serving
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Saccharin tablets contribute zero fat, zero saturated fat, and zero cholesterol per serving, posing no direct cardiovascular lipid risk; however, long-term high-dose saccharin use has been associated in some observational studies with altered gut microbiota and potential metabolic effects, and patients with CVD should use saccharin in moderation as part of a balanced dietary pattern.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: No anti-inflammatory nutrients present; refined sugar from bulking agents may promote low-grade inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Saccharin provides no anti-inflammatory nutrients (zero omega-3, antioxidants, polyphenols, or dietary fibre) and the dextrose bulking agent contributes refined sugar; individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should use saccharin only in trace amounts and prefer anti-inflammatory dietary patterns incorporating whole foods, omega-3 sources, and polyphenol-rich foods.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Saccharin is a synthetic non-caloric artificial sweetener produced by chemical synthesis, commercially available as a white crystalline powder or compressed tablets. It is one of the oldest artificial sweeteners, discovered in 1879, and is approximately 300–400 times sweeter than sucrose. Commercial saccharin products (such as Sweet'N Low) contain sodium saccharin as the active ingredient combined with bulking agents such as dextrose and lactose to give the product measurable volume and tablet form. The HPB Singapore Foodo database records saccharin (F13020002) as containing 360 kcal, 89.11 g carbohydrate, and 85.19 g sugar per 100 g — figures that reflect the dextrose and lactose bulking agents rather than the saccharin molecule itself, which contributes no calories. At a realistic serving size of approximately 0.05 g per tablet, actual caloric, sugar, and sodium contributions are negligible. Saccharin is approved by the US FDA, the American Diabetes Association, and Singapore HPB as a safe sweetener for use in diabetes management. It is classified as food additive E954 under the EU food additive numbering system. Clinically, saccharin does not raise blood glucose and has zero glycemic impact at typical serving quantities, making it suitable for diabetic patients when used as directed. However, some research suggests potential gut microbiome alterations with high-dose use. Saccharin contains no fat, no cholesterol, no dietary fibre, and no anti-inflammatory nutrients, making it nutritionally inert beyond its sweetening function. It is used globally as a tabletop sweetener, in beverages, and in food manufacturing as a sugar substitute.
common_user_queries: How many calories does saccharin have?
Is saccharin safe for high blood pressure?
What is the glycemic index of saccharin?
Is saccharin the same as Sweet'N Low?
Can I use saccharin if I have hypertension?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F13020002), an approved primary source. The record is marked as reviewed with confidence 0.95 in the input. GI is estimated via analogous dextrose-based bulking agent from the University of Sydney GI Database, clearly documented as an analogous estimate.
notes: Fat_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database (F13020002) — saccharin commercial powder contains no fat by formulation; confirmed zero. Saturated_fat_g and trans_fat_g are confirmed zero by formulation. Dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB source. Cholesterol_mg is confirmed zero per HPB source. Calcium_mg is confirmed zero per HPB source. IMPORTANT CLINICAL CAVEAT: The per-100g nutrient values in this record reflect the commercial saccharin product including dextrose and lactose bulking agents, not pure saccharin. At a typical serving size of 0.05 g per tablet, actual sugar intake is approximately 0.043 g, sodium approximately 0.21 mg, and caloric contribution approximately 0.18 kcal — all clinically negligible. The high per-100g sugar (85.19 g) and sodium (428 mg) figures are therefore misleading when assessed without serving-size context. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, water_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: detailed micronutrient and fatty acid breakdown for commercial saccharin powder not available in HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on dextrose bulking agent; at 0.05 g serving, glycemic load is approximately 0.03 — clinically negligible.
